coredata.dc:title: An innovative green sensing strategy based on Cu-doped Tragacanth/Chitosan nano carbon dots for Isoniazid detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shekarbeygi, Zahra
Farhadian, Negin
Ansari, Mohabbat
Shahlaei, Mohsen
Moradi, Sajad
coredata.dc:description: Although Isoniazid (INH) is one of the drugs used as the first line treatment of tuberculosis, its high concentrations in the human body can cause severe complications such as; recurrent seizures, profound metabolic acidosis, coma and even death. Hence it is necessary to designing the sensors capable of detecting very low amounts of drug. A Cu doped Tragacanth/Chitosan carbon dot (CD) with excellent optical properties and photo-thermal stability was synthesized, characterized and used for sensing Isoniazid by a fluorescence Off-ON mechanism based on redox reaction between INH and Fe3+ as quencher. During the first step of reaction, Fe3+ bind to the CDs and due to an electron transfer process the fluorescence intensity of CDs is quenched. There after by introduction of Isoniazid to the CDs-Fe3+ system, Fe3+ converts to Fe2+ and the fluorescence was recovered. Experiments confirm that new method has high ability to detect low concentration of Isoniazid even in the presence of other drugs and interfering materials. In conclusion, this innovative strategy for developing a low cost and sensitive sensor can be used in future health-related programs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensor
Carbon dot
Drug detection
Isoniazid
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Isoniazid by fluorescence spectroscopy
Quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
Fluorescence restoration of CDs–Fe3+ system by Isoniazid
Determination of Isoniazid in blood serum samples
Determination of Isoniazid in waste water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences [grant number of 3006757 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of the bio-polymer based CDs: A) GT-chitosan, B) GT – chitosan-Cu.
The Optical stability of the bio-polymer based CDs during different days.
The thermal stability of the bio-polymer based CDs: A. different temperature, B. different time in 37 °C.
UV–Vis spectroscopy of the bio-polymer based CDs.
The results of DLS analysis for A) GT-chitosan based CDs; B) GT-chitosan-Cu based CDs.
SEM imaging of as synthesis CDs A) GT-chitosan based CDs; B) GT-chitosan-Cu based CDs.
SEM imaging of as synthesis CDs A) GT-chitosan based CDs; B) GT-chitosan-Cu based CDs.
EDX elemental analysis of CDs A) GT-chitosan based CDs; B) GT-chitosan-Cu based CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the bio-polymer based CDs.
XRD patterns of as-prepared CDs A) GT-chitosan and B) GT-chitosan-Cu based CDs.
Cyclic voltammograms of bio-polymer based CDs.
(A) Fluorescence changes of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ and (B) comparing of Fe3+ with other various metal ions for fluorescent decrement.
Fluorescence changes of different CDs in the presence of Fe3+.
Fluorescence spectrums of CDs-Fe3+(A) and CDs-Fe2+ (B) in the presence of increasing Isoniazid (1 × 10− 6 mol·L− 1).

1,10-Phenanthroline discoloration experiments under different conditions (A): Free Isoniazid, (B and C) Free CDs, (D) Free Fe3+, (E) Fe3+/Isoniazid and (F) Free Fe2+, all in complex with 1,10-Phenanthroline.
(A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentrations of Isoniazid, (B) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of Isoniazid (M).

The fluorescence spectra for drug interference of I) rifampin, II) pyrazinamide and III) streptomycin, (A, B, C and D are the spectra related to free CDs, Fe3+ treated CDs, free isoniazid and isoniazid/interfere drug respectively).
The graphical representation of Cd‑iron in the presence of isoniazid.
List of different methods for determine Isoniazid.
Determination of Isoniazid in blood serum samples by the proposed method (n = 5).
Determination of Isoniazid in waste water samples by the proposed method (n = 5).

head.ce:title: An innovative green sensing strategy based on Cu-doped Tragacanth/Chitosan nano carbon dots for Isoniazid detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: S. Moradi, Assistant professor of Nanobiotechnology, Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.
Correspondence to: M. Shahlaei, Associate Professor of Medicinal Chemistry, Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although Isoniazid (INH) is one of the drugs used as the first line treatment of tuberculosis, its high concentrations in the human body can cause severe complications such as; recurrent seizures, profound metabolic acidosis, coma and even death. Hence it is necessary to designing the sensors capable of detecting very low amounts of drug. A Cu doped Tragacanth/Chitosan carbon dot (CD) with excellent optical properties and photo-thermal stability was synthesized, characterized and used for sensing Isoniazid by a fluorescence Off-ON mechanism based on redox reaction between INH and Fe3+ as quencher. During the first step of reaction, Fe3+ bind to the CDs and due to an electron transfer process the fluorescence intensity of CDs is quenched. There after by introduction of Isoniazid to the CDs-Fe3+ system, Fe3+ converts to Fe2+ and the fluorescence was recovered. Experiments confirm that new method has high ability to detect low concentration of Isoniazid even in the presence of other drugs and interfering materials. In conclusion, this innovative strategy for developing a low cost and sensitive sensor can be used in future health-related programs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, Carbon Dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in the world of nanomaterials [1–4]. Compared to the instable conventional organic fluorescent dyes and cytotoxic heavy metal-based quantum dots, CDs were considered as a new topic in many scientific studies because of their excellent physicochemical properties, facile synthesis, good water solubility, low-toxicity, high photo stability, low cost, and excellent biodegradability/biocompatibility [5–8]. Therefore they are used in different practical applications including biosensors [9], gene transmission [10], drug carriers [11–14], pollutant removal [15] and bioimaging probes [16–19]. The extraordinary fluorescence properties of these materials are important for use in analytical chemistry, especially in environmental and biological analyte sensing and imaging [20]. So far, various strategies have been introduced to modify and improve the optical properties of these materials. The literatures show that the applying of metal or non-metal elements containing free electrons such as Nitrogen, Sulfur as well as, Silver, Copper, and Gold into carbon nanostructures is an effective way to tune the electronic structures, increase quantum yield, and improve optical response, fluorescence property, and preventing accumulation of them [21,22].

Isoniazid (INH) as a bacteriostatic agent is widely used for chemotherapy of tuberculosis alone or in combination by other drugs mostly rifampin. It also seems to be active for extrapulmonary illnesses such as meningitis and genitourinary infections [23]. However, a high concentration of this therapeutic agent can lead to epilepsy, liver function failure, and even death [24,25]. On the other hand, depending on the patient's physical condition, from 20 to 70% of the dose of the drug is intact through the urine [26], which eventually enters the wastewater that like other antibiotics, can lead to the emergence of resistant strains of dangerous bacteria such as tuberculosis. This has incite many investigators to use simple, quick, and highly sensitive detection methods for Isoniazid in its pure form both in biological environments and pharmaceutical effluents [27]. There are various analytical procedures for probing of Isoniazid, including titrimetry [28], visible spectrophotometry [29,30], polarography [31], coulometry [32], fluorimetry [33], chemiluminescence [34], and several chromatographic methods [35]. These methods were based on a variety of reaction schemes such as nucleophile substitution [36], condensation [37–39], derivatization [40–42], diazo-coupling [43], oxidative coupling [44], complex formation [45,46], and redox followed by complexation [47–49]. Despite diversity, these methods suffer from severe experimental conditions, the use of organic solvents, weak sensitivity and narrow linear ranges [23]. Recent studies confirm that Isoniazid has a reductant effect on Iron (III) [49] and Cerium(IV) [50]. In various studies, iron (III) was utilized as an oxidizing agent for identification of several drug molecules [51,52] because of its oxidability for several other chemicals [53]. In the other hand recent research studies suggest that increasing Iron (III) to carbon dots can cause quenching fluorescence intensity of CDs [54,55].

Hence there are no reports on applying CDs as a probe reagent in determination of Isoniazid, in this study for the first time a Cu-doped carbon dot were synthesized by using Gum Tragacanth (GT) and Chitosan (CS) biopolymers and this CD was used for the detection of Isoniazid by a new proposed Off-On fluorimetric approach based on a redox reaction between Isoniazid and iron (III).

Chitosan (CS), copper (II) sulfate, FeCl3.6H2O and Isoniazid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Gum-Tragacanth (GT) was prepared as reported in our previous study [56]. In all experiments, deionized water was applied. The GT solution (1% w/v) was prepared by dissolving 2 g of GT powder in 200 ml deionized water, and then kept at ambient temperature for 24 h. Similarly, chitosan solution (1% w/v) was prepared in 0.1% of acetic acid. Three solutions of 0.2 mol·L− 1 copper (II) sulfate, 1 × 10− 3 mol·L− 1 of FeCl3.6H2O and a stock solution of 1 × 10− 4 mol·L− 1 Isoniazid were also prepared.

Fluorescent CDs were prepared using two natural biopolymers of chitosan and gum tragacanth by hydrothermal method. At this step, two types of CDs were synthesized: one of them was made by only using equal amounts of biopolymers and the other was synthesized using biopolymers and copper (II) sulfate. In brief 25 ml of GT (0.5% w/v) and equal amount of CS (0.5%w/v) were mixed and stirred for 10 min. Then 20 ml of ethanol was added to the solution and putted in Teflon-lined autoclave following by incubated at 250 °C for 45 min. In the case of CU doped CDs a 10 ml of CuSO4 (0.2 mol·L− 1) was added to polymer solution. Separation of pure CDs from unreacted materials were done by adding 20 ml of ethanol and centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 15 min and three times. The resulted pellet were dispersed in deionized water and freeze dried for further using.

The adsorption spectra of the synthesized CDs were recorded using an Agilent 8453 (USA) spectrophotometer. To obtain the excitation-emission spectra and determination of Isoniazid in the presence of CDs, a PerkinElmer LS-45 (USA) Spectrofluorimeter was used. A FT-IR Shimadzu IR2 Spectrometer (Japan) was used to identify the functional groups on the CD surface at room temperature. The EDX method was used to analysis the elemental composition of as prepared CDs. The crystal structure of CDs was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Inel Equaniox 3000 (France), 30 kV, 20 mA, K alpha Cu: 1.54 Å. The surface morphology and size of the CDs were determined by scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (TESCAN, Czech Republic) and DLS techniques (Malvern, England). High resolution TEM imaging was done using (Hitachi H-9500). The reduction and oxidation potentials of the GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) using a Potentiostat Metrohm μlab III (Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Isoniazid by fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: To study the quenching effect of Fe3+ on CDs fluorescence intensity, at first, a series of Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations were prepared and added to CDs solution. By changing the amount of ferric ion, a concentration of Fe3+ has been identified, which can able to reduce the CDs fluorescence to its lowest possible extent. In the next step, florescence restoration of CDs–Fe3+ system was performed by adding Isoniazid. For this purpose different concentrations of drug were added to the CDs–Fe3+ solution.

For real sample analysis serum samples were treated with 6 ml of methanol as a serum protein precipitating agent. After vortexing for 150 s, the precipitated proteins were separated by centrifugation for 6 min at 15000 rpm. The clear supernatant layer was filtrated through a 0.2 μm Milli-pore filter to obtain a protein-free human serum. To determine the concentration of INH in real sample 1 ml of protein free serum added to 20 ml of PBS buffer (0.1 M) and PH = 7.4. Then 15 μl of isoniazid (1 mM) solution was spiked to the serum.

A concentration of 1 μg/ml of CDs was first quenched by adding 10 μl of Fe3+ with the final concentration of 1.5 × 10− 4 μM. From the prepared serum sample, 3 μl was added to the CD-Fe3+ system and the emission spectrum was recorded at 465 nm. The data was then subjected to those of standard curve and the concentration of INH was measured.

The fluorescence spectrums of GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1A, the excitation and maximum emission wavelengths for GT/CS based CDs are observed at 340 nm and 440 nm, respectively which is right shifting to 360 nm and 465 in GT/CS/Cu based CDs(Fig. 1B). These results in agreements with those of Uv-Vis spectroscopy confirms that in the presence of Cu the HOMO-LOMO band gap of CDs is decreased significantly.

As the most important property of any fluorophore, the quantum yield (Φ) of the CDs as the ratio of the number of photons emitted to photons absorbed was calculated based on following equation [57]:
Φ CDs
=
Φ S
×
F CDs
F S
×
A S
A CDs

The values of quantum yield for GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu carbon dots were 44% and 55%, respectively which clearly indicating the positive effect of Cu on the optical properties of the CDs. The higher quantum yield for GT/CS/Cu based CDs can be attributed to the presence of the free electrons in the copper element which increases quantum efficiency and improves optical responses. Another important feature of such a fluorescence material is the stability of their optical properties under conditions of varying temperature and light. Fluorophore materials undergo chemical changes over time and exposure to light or temperature leading to photo bleaching. The stability of the as-synthesized CDs was studied in terms of thermal and photostability. The photostability of CDs was investigated by daily sampling during 14 days and results are presented in Fig. 2 . After 14 days, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 15% and 5% for GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs, respectively. These slight changes are indicative of high photo stability of the as-synthesized CDs.

The thermal stability of the both CDs was investigated by examining the effect of temperature changes on their life span or investigating their stability in selected temperature for different times. As can be seen from Fig. 3A, during 10 min of incubation time, by increasing the temperature to 55 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs reduces by 52% and 33%, respectively. Also the fluorescence intensity of the as-synthesized CDs reaches half of its initial value after 60 min in 37 °C as shown in Fig. 3B.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the absorption spectrum of the GT/CS-based CDs shows strong peaks located at 250–300 nm showing π–π* transitions. These peaks further confirm the presence of CC functional groups in the bio-polymer based CDs surface which make right shifting after addition of Cu [58].

The zeta potential (ζ) measurement is a way of ensuring the stability of colloidal system of nanoparticles. The GT/CS based CDs has a negative zeta potential (−17.97 and −21.6 mV) as a results of the carboxylic surface functional groups. The average particle sizes (hydrodynamic diameter) of 87 and 68 nm were measured by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique for GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs, respectively (Fig. 5 ).

Fig. 6 displays the SEM images of both as-synthesized CDs. These images show that CDs are spherical in shape and the average particle size of GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs are 22.7 nm and 17.44 nm, respectively. The particle size of GT/CS/Cu based CDs is less and more monodisperse than the GT/CS based CDs. Moreover the TEM images were used to determine the precise size of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 7 the exact sizes of particles are 8.7 and 6.2 nm respectively.
The elemental analysis of the both CDs which determined by EDX analysis are presented in Fig. 8 . The uniform presence of copper in a GT/CS/Cu based CDs can be seen from this results.

From the FTIR spectra that are shown in Fig. 9 , the functional groups presents at the surface of CDs were identified. The characteristic peaks at 3400, 2900, and 1600 cm− 1 are respectively ascribed to the symmetric stretching of OH, CH and CC which are present in both GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs. Another three peaks are located at 1100, 700 and 510 cm− 1 and can be belongs to the Cu-aromatic rings, CuC and CuO bonds respectively and confirms the presence of copper in complex with organic moieties of GT/CS/Cu based CDs. Other absorption peak placed at 1710 is attributed to the stretching of CO in carbonyl element of carboxylic acid group for both GT/CS and GT/CS/Cu based CDs which increases theirs solubility and stability in water and grant them to have a total negative charge [59,60].

The XRD patterns of both CDs are depicted in Fig. 10 . A broad peak at 22° in the XRD patterns represents the graphite plates which are wider than normal due many strains from polymer side chains. In the case of the XRD patterns for GT/CS/Cu based CDs, a series of sharp peaks in regions between 15° and 50° are related to the different copper compartments including Cu, CuO [61].

The electrochemical behavior of both CDs is shown in Fig. 11 . The results indicate that GT/CS/Cu based CDs have higher electrical activity than GT/CS based CDs which caused more redox activity. By probing the potential from negative to positive values two distinct oxidation peaks are observed in Cu-doped CDs which are not appears in Cu free CDs. The peaks can be related to oxidation of Cu and are located in 0.01 and 0.03v.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12.A presents the quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of GT/CS/Cu based CDs. In this regard, different concentrations of Fe3+ (1 × 10− 6-1.5 × 10− 4 μM) were added to CDs solution and the fluorescence intensity changes have been reported in the presence of these values. A concentration of 1.5 × 10− 4 M Fe 3+ was chooses as optimal value because at this amount the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was reduced by half its initial value. The results of recent studies show that the photoluminescence quenching by Fe3+ occurs because of a fast electron transfer process between Fe3+ and CDs [62–64]. Fe3+ was capable to absorb on the surface of CDs and fit with different functional groups on the surface of CDs. According to this interaction, electron in the excited state of CDs would transfer to the unfilled orbit of Fe3+, and leads to the creation of a new non-radiative electron/hole recombination that ultimately causes fluorescence quenching. Some previous studies have also reported a significant reduction in CDs fluorescence in the presence of Fe3+ [22,65]. In general, the low electronegativity of Cu at the CD surface leads to an increase in the density of surface electrons over the oxygen atoms thus forming coordination interaction with Fe3+, and ultimately causes more fluorescence intensity to be quenched [22]. Additionally Fig. 12.B shows the changes of the CDs fluorescence reduction ratio in the presence of various metal ions. As can be seen in this figure, the CDs fluorescence intensity is most susceptible to the ferric ion, and this sensitivity cannot be seen even with ferrous ion. Fig. 13 shows the comparison of the fluorescence reduction for the two synthesized CDs versus the same concentration of Fe3+. This comparison shows that the presence of Cu resulted in a further decrease of the CDs fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence restoration of CDs–Fe3+ system by Isoniazid
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14.A displays the restoration of the CDs–Fe3+ fluorescence in the presence of Isoniazid. The experimental results showed that Fe3+ adsorption on the CDs surface caused the quenching of its fluorescence. As a result of the addition of Isoniazid to the CDs-Fe3+ system, by a redox reaction the Fe3+ is converted to Fe2+. Since CDs fluorescence is not sensitive to Fe2+ thereby the fluorescence restoration occurs as a function of INH concentration (Fig. 14.A).

However Fig. 14.A shows the reduction of CDs fluorescence due to the introduction of Fe3+ and its recovery in the presence of Isoniazid. Also Fig. 14B confirms that these interactions are limited to Fe3+, and Fe2+ does not make such changes. The effect of adding the Isoniazid to the Fe3+-CDs system is graphically illustrated in Scheme 1 [49].

As an easy assay for detection of Fe (II) we use the 1,10-phenanthroline ligand which its interaction with Fe (II) forms an orange-red complex. The results are presented in Fig. 15 and confirm that only in the presence of Fe(II) or Isoniazid/Fe(III) the change in color is observed. No one of free isoniazid, free Fe(III) or Free CDs could change the color which proved the formation of Fe(II) from Fe(III) in the presence of Isoniazid. Also it must be noted that neither the free Isoniazid nor free Fe(II) were capable of affecting the CDs fluorescence.

As shown in the Fig. 16 , for quantification of the results, the calibration curve were obtained by adding various concentrations of Isoniazid to the CDs-Fe3+ system and checking the changes in the florescence intensity. A high linear correlation (R2 = 0.995) is observed over the concentration range of 9.00 × 10− 9–1.00 × 10− 6 mol·L− 1 and the linear regression equation is I = 2 × 108C + 360.98.

To check the accuracy of the proposed method, the fluorescence of the CDs-Fe3+-Isoniazid system has been determined for 10 times and the low relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) and detection limit of 0.47% and 8.4 nmol·L− 1 were obtained, respectively.

The results indicate that the CDs-Fe3+ system can detect low concentrations of the Isoniazid. The comparison of the proposed method with the previous methods used to detection of Isoniazid is presented in Table 1 . Results indicate that the linear range and detection limit of the proposed method are far lower than the previous ones.

In order to evaluate the ability of the proposed method for Isoniazid detection, the interference effect of other drugs that are associated with Isoniazid were also investigated. In this regard samples containing a constant amount of Isoniazid (1.00 × 10− 6 mol·L− 1) and various concentrations of Rifampin, Pyrazinamide and Streptomycin were prepared and evaluated. The tolerance limit was considered as ±3%. The results of the interference experiment are shown in the Fig. 17 . The figure indicating there is no any changes in isoniazid spectra after addition of rifampin, pyrazinamide and streptomycin. It also confirms that other drugs associated with Isoniazid therapeutic regiments do not interact with the proposed method for the Isoniazid determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Isoniazid in blood serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practical applicability of the proposed method in clinical specimens, a defined system of CDs-Fe3+ was applied to detection of Isoniazid in human serum samples. A certain concentrations of Isoniazid were spiked to the serum samples and then its concentration was determined by proposed method. As can be seen from Table 2 , a good match was found between the measurements carried out by the proposed method and the actual concentrations of the Isoniazid. Thus it can be concluded that new proposed probe are feasible to detect Isoniazid in biological environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Isoniazid in waste water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the proposed method in real samples, a system defined by CDs-Fe3+ was used for analysis of waste water samples. By a tolerance limit of the co-usage drugs of Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide and Streptomycin was added to three samples of waste waters. Then the different concentrations of isoniazid were added to the samples, and the fluorescence intensity of which were recorded. The results of the new calculated concentrations are shown in Table 3 and as reported in the table, the results of Isoniazid detected in each sample is consistent with initial spiked values. These results also indicating the potent of the nano sensor for more accurate drug detection in the waste water samples in comparison with serum ones.

In this study, two fluorescent CDs were prepared by natural biopolymers using environmental friendly and simple conventional hydrothermal method. To prepare one of them in addition to GT and CS biopolymers, metal salt (Cu2SO4) was used as doping agent to improve optical/photovoltaic properties and increase quantum yield efficiency. CDs based on GT/CS/Cu showed better photoluminescence features than other one. This new CDs was used to Isoniazid detection by the help of an oxidation conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+. Results showed that GT/CS/Cu has a good potential for detecting very low amounts of Isoniazid even in the biological serum and waste water samples. The sensitivity and selectivity of the biosensor for Isoniazid detection were also proved when other co-usage drugs were added to the system. Ultimately, it can be concluded that this new composite can be introduced as a green nano sensor for the identification of Isoniazid.

Zahra Shekarbeygi, Negin Farhadian and Mohabbat Ansari contributed in experiments. Sajad Moradi and Mohsen Shahlaei supported the work and contributed in analyzing the results and writing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors confirmed there is no any conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences [grant number of 3006757].
